cheatSheet:
# Kubernetes & Docker Quick Reference

## Docker Core Concepts

### Images & Containers
- **Image**: Read-only template (layers). Built from a Dockerfile.
- **Container**: Running instance of an image. Isolated process.
- `docker build -t myapp:1.0 .` — Build image from Dockerfile
- `docker run -d -p 8080:80 --name web myapp:1.0` — Run container
- `docker exec -it web bash` — Shell into running container
- `docker ps` / `docker ps -a` — List running / all containers
- `docker images` — List local images
- `docker logs -f web` — Follow container logs
- `docker stop web && docker rm web` — Stop and remove
- `docker rmi myapp:1.0` — Remove image

### Dockerfile Key Instructions
```
FROM node:20-alpine          # Base image
WORKDIR /app                 # Set working directory
COPY package*.json ./        # Copy files (layer cache optimization)
RUN npm ci --only=production # Execute command in new layer
COPY . .                     # Copy rest of source
EXPOSE 3000                  # Document port (informational)
ENV NODE_ENV=production      # Set environment variable
ARG BUILD_VERSION            # Build-time argument
USER node                    # Run as non-root
CMD ["node", "server.js"]    # Default command
ENTRYPOINT ["npm", "start"] # Non-overridable entry
HEALTHCHECK CMD curl -f http://localhost:3000/health || exit 1
```

### Layer Caching Strategy
- Copy dependency files BEFORE source code
- Each RUN/COPY/ADD creates a new layer
- Unchanged layers are cached — order matters
- Use `.dockerignore` to exclude node_modules, .git, etc.

### Multi-Stage Builds
```dockerfile
FROM node:20 AS builder
RUN npm ci && npm run build

FROM node:20-alpine AS runner
COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist
CMD ["node", "dist/index.js"]
```

## Docker Networking
- **bridge**: Default. Containers on same bridge can communicate.
- **host**: Container shares host network stack (Linux only).
- **overlay**: Multi-host networking for Swarm/K8s.
- **none**: Disable networking.
- `docker network create mynet` — Create custom bridge
- `docker run --network mynet myapp` — Attach to network
- Containers on same network resolve by name: `http://api:3000`

## Docker Volumes
- **Named Volume**: `docker volume create pgdata` — Docker managed, persists
- **Bind Mount**: `-v $(pwd)/data:/app/data` — Host path mapped
- **tmpfs**: `--tmpfs /tmp` — In-memory, not persisted
- `docker volume ls` / `docker volume inspect pgdata`

## Docker Compose
```yaml
services:
  api:
    build: .
    ports: ["3000:3000"]
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
    environment:
      - DATABASE_URL=postgres://user:pass@db:5432/mydb
  db:
    image: postgres:16
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "pg_isready", "-U", "user"]
      interval: 5s
      retries: 5
volumes:
  pgdata:
```
- `docker compose up -d` — Start in background
- `docker compose down -v` — Stop and remove volumes
- `docker compose logs -f api` — Follow service logs

## Kubernetes Architecture

### Control Plane
| Component | Role |
|-----------|------|
| **kube-apiserver** | Single entry point; REST API for all K8s operations |
| **etcd** | Distributed key-value store; source of truth for cluster state |
| **kube-scheduler** | Assigns pods to nodes based on resources/constraints |
| **kube-controller-manager** | Runs controllers: ReplicaSet, Deployment, Node, etc. |
| **cloud-controller-manager** | Interfaces with cloud providers (AWS/GCP/Azure) |

### Worker Node Components
| Component | Role |
|-----------|------|
| **kubelet** | Node agent; ensures containers in pods are running |
| **kube-proxy** | Network rules; implements Service load balancing |
| **Container Runtime** | Docker/containerd/CRI-O — actually runs containers |

## Kubernetes Objects

### Pod
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: myapp
  labels:
    app: myapp
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:1.0
    ports:
    - containerPort: 3000
    resources:
      requests: {cpu: 100m, memory: 128Mi}
      limits: {cpu: 500m, memory: 512Mi}
```

### Deployment
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
spec:
  replicas: 3
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate: {maxSurge: 1, maxUnavailable: 0}
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: myapp:1.0
```

### Service Types
- **ClusterIP**: Internal only. Default.
- **NodePort**: Exposes on each node's IP at static port 30000-32767.
- **LoadBalancer**: Provisions cloud LB. External access.
- **ExternalName**: DNS alias to external service.

### Ingress
```yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
spec:
  rules:
  - host: api.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: api-service
            port: {number: 80}
```

### ConfigMaps & Secrets
```bash
kubectl create configmap app-config --from-literal=ENV=prod
kubectl create secret generic db-secret --from-literal=PASSWORD=s3cret
```

### RBAC
```yaml
kind: Role          # Namespace-scoped
kind: ClusterRole   # Cluster-scoped
kind: RoleBinding   # Bind Role to subject
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
```

### Helm
- **Chart**: Package of K8s manifests
- `helm install myapp ./chart` — Deploy chart
- `helm upgrade myapp ./chart --set image.tag=2.0`
- `helm rollback myapp 1` — Roll back to revision 1
- `helm list` / `helm status myapp`

## Essential kubectl Commands
```bash
kubectl get pods -n mynamespace
kubectl describe pod mypod
kubectl logs mypod -c mycontainer
kubectl exec -it mypod -- bash
kubectl apply -f manifest.yaml
kubectl delete -f manifest.yaml
kubectl rollout status deployment/myapp
kubectl rollout undo deployment/myapp
kubectl scale deployment myapp --replicas=5
kubectl port-forward svc/myapp 8080:80
kubectl top nodes / kubectl top pods
```

sessions.title: Docker Fundamentals — Images, Containers, and Dockerfiles
sessions.content:
## Docker Fundamentals ```mermaid graph TB DF[Dockerfile] -->|docker build| IMG[Image
Layered FS] IMG -->|docker run| C1[Container 1] IMG -->|docker run| C2[Container 2] IMG -->|docker push| REG[Registry
Docker Hub / ECR] REG -->|docker pull| IMG2[Image on Host 2] subgraph Docker Engine DAEMON[dockerd] --> CRTD[containerd] CRTD --> RUNC[runc] RUNC --> C1 RUNC --> C2 end ``` Docker is a containerization platform that packages applications and their dependencies into isolated, portable units called containers. Unlike virtual machines, containers share the host kernel — making them lightweight, fast to start, and efficient. ### Core Concepts **Image**: An immutable, layered read-only template. Think of it as a class definition. **Container**: A running instance of an image. Think of it as an object instantiated from a class. **Layer**: Each instruction in a Dockerfile creates a new read-only layer. Layers are cached and shared between images. **Registry**: A storage and distribution system for images. Docker Hub is the public default; AWS ECR, GCR, and GHCR are popular private options. ### Your First Dockerfile — Node.js API ```dockerfile # Stage 1: Install dependencies (using full image for build tools) FROM node:20-alpine AS base WORKDIR /app # Copy ONLY package files first — layer cache optimization # This layer is only invalidated when package.json changes COPY package.json package-lock.json ./ RUN npm ci --only=production # Now copy application source — this layer invalidates on any code change COPY src/ ./src/ # Set runtime configuration ENV NODE_ENV=production ENV PORT=3000 # Document the port (informational — does not actually publish) EXPOSE 3000 # Run as non-root user for security USER node # CMD is the default command — can be overridden at runtime CMD ["node", "src/index.js"] ``` ### Multi-Stage Build — Production Best Practice Multi-stage builds let you use a fat builder image to compile/bundle, then copy only the artifacts into a slim runtime image. This dramatically reduces final image size. ```dockerfile # --- Build Stage --- FROM node:20 AS builder WORKDIR /app COPY package*.json ./ RUN npm ci COPY . . # Build TypeScript / bundle with webpack etc. RUN npm run build # --- Production Stage --- # Only this stage ends up in the final image FROM node:20-alpine AS runner WORKDIR /app # Copy ONLY the compiled output and production deps COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist COPY --from=builder /app/node_modules ./node_modules ENV NODE_ENV=production USER node CMD ["node", "dist/index.js"] ``` Result: A 1.2 GB builder image becomes a 180 MB production image. The build tools, source maps, TypeScript compiler, and dev dependencies are all discarded. ### Dockerfile Instructions Reference | Instruction | Purpose | Example | |-------------|---------|--------| | `FROM` | Base image | `FROM python:3.12-slim` | | `WORKDIR` | Set working directory | `WORKDIR /app` | | `COPY` | Copy files from host | `COPY src/ ./src/` | | `ADD` | Like COPY + can extract tarballs | `ADD archive.tar.gz /app/` | | `RUN` | Execute command in new layer | `RUN pip install -r requirements.txt` | | `ENV` | Set environment variable | `ENV DEBUG=false` | | `ARG` | Build-time variable | `ARG VERSION=latest` | | `EXPOSE` | Document network port | `EXPOSE 8080` | | `USER` | Set running user | `USER nobody` | | `CMD` | Default command (overridable) | `CMD ["gunicorn", "app:app"]` | | `ENTRYPOINT` | Fixed entry point | `ENTRYPOINT ["python"]` | | `HEALTHCHECK` | Container health probe | `HEALTHCHECK CMD curl -f http://localhost/health` | | `VOLUME` | Declare mount point | `VOLUME ["/data"]` | ### Python Example — FastAPI Application ```dockerfile FROM python:3.12-slim AS builder WORKDIR /app # Install build dependencies RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends gcc COPY requirements.txt . RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --user -r requirements.txt # --- Runtime --- FROM python:3.12-slim WORKDIR /app # Copy only installed packages from builder COPY --from=builder /root/.local /root/.local COPY . . ENV PATH=/root/.local/bin:$PATH EXPOSE 8000 HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=5s --start-period=10s \ CMD python -c "import urllib.request; urllib.request.urlopen('http://localhost:8000/health')" CMD ["uvicorn", "main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"] ``` ### Layer Caching — The Golden Rule Docker processes Dockerfile instructions top-to-bottom. When a layer changes, **all subsequent layers are invalidated**. The strategy is: least-frequently-changing instructions first. ``` BAD order: GOOD order: 1. COPY . . 1. COPY requirements.txt . 2. RUN pip install 2. RUN pip install <- cached unless requirements.txt changes 3. ... 3. COPY . . <- only this and below invalidated on code change ``` ### The .dockerignore File Always create `.dockerignore` to prevent bloating the build context: ``` node_modules/ .git/ *.log .env dist/ .DS_Store __pycache__/ *.pyc .pytest_cache/ coverage/ ``` ### Essential Docker CLI Commands ```bash # Build with tag docker build -t myapi:1.0 . docker build -t myapi:1.0 --build-arg VERSION=1.0 . # Run interactively / in background docker run -it --rm ubuntu bash docker run -d -p 8080:3000 --name api myapi:1.0 # Inspect and debug docker ps -a docker logs api -f --tail 100 docker exec -it api sh docker inspect api docker stats # Cleanup docker stop api && docker rm api docker image prune -a # Remove unused images docker system prune # Nuclear option — remove everything unused ``` ### Interview Talking Points - "I always use multi-stage builds in production to minimize image size and attack surface." - "Layer caching is the biggest Dockerfile performance lever — I order instructions from least to most frequently changing." - "I run containers as non-root and use HEALTHCHECK so orchestrators know if the app is actually ready." - "The difference between CMD and ENTRYPOINT: CMD can be overridden at runtime, ENTRYPOINT cannot. I use ENTRYPOINT for the binary and CMD for default arguments."
objectives: Explain the difference between images, containers, and layers
Write a production-quality multi-stage Dockerfile
Apply layer caching optimization strategies
Use Docker CLI to build, run, inspect, and debug containers

activities.description: Write a multi-stage Dockerfile for a Node.js TypeScript API. Build it, run it, exec into it, and verify the healthcheck passes. Then deliberately break layer cache ordering and measure build time difference.
Take an existing Python Flask app and containerize it. Add a .dockerignore, HEALTHCHECK, and ensure it runs as a non-root user. Verify with 'docker inspect' that the user is not root.

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between CMD and ENTRYPOINT in a Dockerfile?:::CMD sets the default command and arguments that can be completely overridden when you run 'docker run myimage custom-command'. ENTRYPOINT sets the fixed executable that always runs — it cannot be overridden (only with --entrypoint flag). ENTRYPOINT is typically used for the binary (e.g., ENTRYPOINT ["nginx"]) while CMD provides default arguments (e.g., CMD ["-g", "daemon off;"]). Together: docker run myimage -c /etc/nginx/nginx.conf would pass '-c /etc/nginx/nginx.conf' as arguments to nginx.

Why do we copy package.json before copying source files in a Node.js Dockerfile?:::Docker caches each layer. If you copy all source files first (COPY . .), any code change invalidates that layer and everything after it, forcing npm install to re-run every time. By copying only package.json first and running npm install, that expensive layer is cached and reused on all subsequent builds as long as package.json has not changed. Only when dependencies change does npm install re-run. This can save 30-120 seconds per build in CI.

What is a multi-stage build and why does it matter for production?:::A multi-stage build uses multiple FROM instructions in one Dockerfile. Each stage can use a different base image. You use a fat builder image (with compilers, dev tools) for build steps, then copy only the compiled artifacts into a slim runtime image. The final image contains none of the build tools, reducing size (often 5-10x smaller) and attack surface. Example: a TypeScript app compiled in node:20 (1.2GB) deployed in node:20-alpine (180MB).

What is .dockerignore and what should always be in it?:::A .dockerignore file tells Docker which files and directories to exclude from the build context sent to the Docker daemon. Without it, 'docker build' sends everything including node_modules (hundreds of MB), .git history, local .env files with secrets, and test artifacts. Always include: node_modules/, .git/, .env*, dist/, __pycache__/, *.log, coverage/, .DS_Store. Smaller build context means faster builds and no accidental secret leakage into images.
sessions.successCriteria: Can write a production multi-stage Dockerfile from memory, explain layer caching optimization, and use Docker CLI to build, debug, and manage containers

sessions.title: Docker Networking & Volumes — Persistence and Communication
sessions.content:
## Docker Networking & Volumes ```mermaid graph LR Client[Client] --> ING[Ingress
nginx/traefik] ING -->|/api| SVC1[Service: api
ClusterIP] ING -->|/web| SVC2[Service: web
ClusterIP] SVC1 --> P1[Pod api-1] SVC1 --> P2[Pod api-2] SVC2 --> P3[Pod web-1] SVC2 --> P4[Pod web-2] ``` By default, containers are isolated — they cannot talk to each other or persist data. Networking and volumes solve these two fundamental problems. ### Docker Networking Modes #### 1. Bridge Network (Default) Each container gets its own virtual network interface. Containers on the same bridge network can communicate by container name (Docker's built-in DNS). Containers on different bridge networks are isolated. ```bash # Default bridge — containers cannot resolve each other by name docker run --name api myapi docker run --name db postgres # api cannot reach 'db' by name on default bridge # Custom bridge — enables DNS resolution docker network create myapp-net docker run --name api --network myapp-net myapi docker run --name db --network myapp-net postgres # Now api can connect to 'db:5432' — Docker DNS resolves 'db' ``` #### 2. Host Network The container bypasses Docker's network stack entirely and uses the host's IP and ports directly. No NAT, maximum performance, but no isolation. ```bash docker run --network host nginx # nginx listens on host's port 80 directly — no -p mapping needed/possible ``` Use cases: High-performance networking (game servers, monitoring agents), Linux-only (not available on macOS/Windows Docker Desktop). #### 3. Overlay Network Spans multiple Docker hosts. Used by Docker Swarm and Kubernetes for cross-node pod communication. Encrypted by default with `--opt encrypted`. ```bash docker network create --driver overlay --attachable myswarm-net ``` #### 4. None Disables all networking. The container has only a loopback interface. ```bash docker run --network none alpine # Completely isolated ``` #### 5. macvlan / ipvlan Assigns a real MAC/IP address to the container — it appears as a physical device on the network. Used for legacy apps that expect to be on the physical network. ### Network Commands ```bash docker network ls # List networks docker network create --driver bridge app-net # Create custom bridge docker network inspect app-net # Detailed info, connected containers docker network connect app-net mycontainer # Connect running container docker network disconnect app-net mycontainer docker network rm app-net # Remove network ``` ### Practical Networking: Multi-Container Architecture ```bash # Create isolated networks — api can reach db, but db cannot reach frontend docker network create frontend-net docker network create backend-net docker run -d --name frontend --network frontend-net -p 80:80 mynginx docker run -d --name api --network frontend-net myapi # Also join backend docker network connect backend-net api docker run -d --name db --network backend-net postgres # frontend -> api (via frontend-net) -> db (via backend-net) # frontend CANNOT reach db directly ``` ### Docker Volumes — Data Persistence Containers are ephemeral — when a container is removed, its filesystem is gone. Volumes solve this. #### 1. Named Volumes (Recommended for Production) Docker manages the storage location. Data persists across container restarts and removals. ```bash # Create a named volume docker volume create pgdata # Use it docker run -d \ --name postgres \ -v pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data \ -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=secret \ postgres:16 # Volume persists even after: docker stop postgres && docker rm postgres # Start a new container — data is still there docker run -d --name postgres2 -v pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data postgres:16 # Volume management docker volume ls docker volume inspect pgdata docker volume rm pgdata # Only works if no container is using it docker volume prune # Remove all unused volumes ``` #### 2. Bind Mounts — Host Path Mapping Maps a specific host directory into the container. Useful for development (live code reload) but less portable than named volumes. ```bash # Mount current directory into container (development mode) docker run -d \ --name api-dev \ -v $(pwd)/src:/app/src \ -v $(pwd)/config:/app/config:ro \ -p 3000:3000 \ myapi:dev # :ro = read-only mount — container cannot write to config # Python development with hot reload docker run -d \ --name fastapi-dev \ -v $(pwd):/app \ -p 8000:8000 \ myapp:dev \ uvicorn main:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 ``` #### 3. tmpfs Mounts — In-Memory Storage Mounted in RAM. Not persisted. Ideal for sensitive data like session tokens that should never touch disk. ```bash docker run -d \ --name secure-api \ --tmpfs /tmp:size=100m,mode=1777 \ --tmpfs /app/sessions:size=50m \ myapi ``` ### Volume in Dockerfile vs Runtime ```dockerfile # VOLUME instruction declares a mount point # Docker creates an anonymous volume here automatically VOLUME ["/app/uploads", "/app/logs"] # WARNING: Anonymous volumes are hard to manage — prefer named volumes at runtime ``` ### Real-World Volume Patterns ```bash # Database with backup volume docker run -d \ --name mysql \ -v mysql-data:/var/lib/mysql \ -v $(pwd)/backup:/backup \ -e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=secret \ mysql:8 # Backup: exec into container and dump to bind-mounted backup dir docker exec mysql \ mysqldump -u root -psecret mydb > /backup/mydb-$(date +%Y%m%d).sql # Redis with persistence docker run -d \ --name redis \ -v redis-data:/data \ redis:7-alpine redis-server --save 60 1 --appendonly yes ``` ### Volume vs Bind Mount — When to Use Which | Scenario | Recommendation | |----------|---------------| | Database data | Named Volume — Docker manages it | | Dev live reload | Bind Mount — maps your source code | | Config files | Bind Mount (read-only) | | Shared between containers | Named Volume | | Sensitive temp data | tmpfs | | CI/CD (no persistence needed) | Anonymous volume or none | ### Port Publishing Deep Dive ```bash # -p hostPort:containerPort docker run -p 8080:80 nginx # host:8080 -> container:80 docker run -p 127.0.0.1:8080:80 nginx # Only bind on localhost docker run -p 80 nginx # Docker picks a random host port # View port mappings docker port mycontainer ``` ### Interview Talking Points - "I always create custom bridge networks — named DNS resolution and isolation are essential." - "Named volumes for databases, bind mounts only for local development. Never anonymous volumes in production." - "tmpfs is my go-to for session data and temp secrets — nothing sensitive should hit disk." - "Network segmentation in Docker: frontend-net and backend-net so the web tier cannot directly reach the database."
objectives: Explain bridge, host, overlay, and none networking modes with use cases
Create and use custom bridge networks for inter-container DNS resolution
Distinguish between named volumes, bind mounts, and tmpfs with appropriate use cases
Implement network segmentation for multi-tier applications

activities.description: Create a three-container setup (web, api, db) with two networks: frontend-net (web+api) and backend-net (api+db). Verify that web can reach api but cannot reach db directly using docker exec and curl/ping.
Run a PostgreSQL container with a named volume. Stop and remove the container. Start a new container with the same volume and verify data persists. Then create a backup using a bind mount.

reviewQuestions: Why should you create a custom bridge network instead of using the default bridge?:::The default bridge network does not support DNS resolution between containers — you must use IP addresses. Custom bridge networks have built-in DNS: containers can reach each other by container name (e.g., 'db:5432'). Custom bridges also provide better isolation: only containers explicitly connected to the network can communicate. The default bridge connects all containers on the same Docker host, which is a security risk.

What is the difference between a named volume and a bind mount?:::A named volume is managed entirely by Docker. Docker decides where to store the data (typically /var/lib/docker/volumes/). It is portable, works the same on all systems, and is the recommended approach for production data. A bind mount maps a specific host path (e.g., $(pwd)/data) into the container. The container sees the host files, and the host sees container writes. Bind mounts are ideal for development (live reload) but are not portable — they depend on a specific host path existing.

When would you use tmpfs instead of a volume?:::tmpfs mounts data into RAM only — it is never written to disk and disappears when the container stops. Use it for: session tokens and auth credentials that should not be logged or stored, temporary processing data for compliance reasons (GDPR/HIPAA), and high-speed temporary scratch space. The security benefit is that sensitive data cannot be recovered from disk after the container exits.

How does Docker container-to-container networking work on a custom bridge network?:::Docker runs an embedded DNS server for each custom bridge network. When containers join the network, they register their container name (and any aliases) with this DNS server. When one container makes a DNS query for another container name, Docker's DNS resolves it to that container's internal IP. This means you can hardcode 'db:5432' or 'redis:6379' in your application config and it will always resolve correctly regardless of which IP Docker assigned.
sessions.successCriteria: Can design a multi-tier Docker network topology with proper isolation, and correctly choose between named volumes, bind mounts, and tmpfs for different data persistence scenarios

sessions.title: Docker Compose — Multi-Container Application Orchestration
sessions.content:
## Docker Compose

Docker Compose lets you define and run multi-container applications using a single YAML file. Instead of running complex docker run commands, you declare your entire application stack — services, networks, volumes, environment variables, health checks — in `docker-compose.yml`.

### Complete Production-Ready docker-compose.yml

Here is a realistic full-stack application: React frontend, Node.js API, PostgreSQL database, Redis cache, and Nginx reverse proxy.

```yaml
version: '3.9'

services:
  # --- Reverse Proxy ---
  nginx:
    image: nginx:1.25-alpine
    ports:
      - "80:80"
      - "443:443"
    volumes:
      - ./nginx/nginx.conf:/etc/nginx/nginx.conf:ro
      - ./nginx/ssl:/etc/nginx/ssl:ro
    depends_on:
      api:
        condition: service_healthy
    networks:
      - frontend
    restart: unless-stopped

  # --- API Service ---
  api:
    build:
      context: ./api
      dockerfile: Dockerfile
      target: runner          # Use specific stage in multi-stage build
    environment:
      NODE_ENV: production
      DATABASE_URL: postgres://appuser:${DB_PASSWORD}@db:5432/appdb
      REDIS_URL: redis://redis:6379
      JWT_SECRET: ${JWT_SECRET}
    env_file:
      - .env.production       # Load additional vars from file
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
      redis:
        condition: service_healthy
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "wget", "-qO-", "http://localhost:3000/health"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 3
      start_period: 20s
    networks:
      - frontend
      - backend
    restart: unless-stopped
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          cpus: '0.5'
          memory: 512M

  # --- PostgreSQL Database ---
  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: appuser
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ${DB_PASSWORD}
      POSTGRES_DB: appdb
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
      - ./db/init:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d:ro  # Run SQL scripts on first start
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U appuser -d appdb"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 5
      start_period: 10s
    networks:
      - backend
    restart: unless-stopped

  # --- Redis Cache ---
  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    command: redis-server --save 60 1 --loglevel warning --requirepass ${REDIS_PASSWORD}
    volumes:
      - redis-data:/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "redis-cli", "--no-auth-warning", "-a", "${REDIS_PASSWORD}", "ping"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 5
    networks:
      - backend
    restart: unless-stopped

  # --- Database Migration (one-shot service) ---
  migrate:
    build:
      context: ./api
    command: npm run migrate
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: postgres://appuser:${DB_PASSWORD}@db:5432/appdb
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
    networks:
      - backend
    restart: on-failure  # Retry until migrations succeed

networks:
  frontend:
    driver: bridge
  backend:
    driver: bridge
    internal: true    # No external internet access — only internal traffic

volumes:
  pgdata:
    driver: local
  redis-data:
    driver: local
```

### Key Compose Concepts Explained

#### depends_on with Conditions

```yaml
depends_on:
  db:
    condition: service_healthy   # Wait for healthcheck to pass
  cache:
    condition: service_started   # Just wait for container to start (default)
  migration:
    condition: service_completed_successfully  # Wait for one-shot service to exit 0
```

#### Environment Variables — Three Patterns

```yaml
environment:
  # Pattern 1: Inline value
  NODE_ENV: production
  
  # Pattern 2: Reference from host environment or .env file
  DB_PASSWORD: ${DB_PASSWORD}
  
  # Pattern 3: Pass-through from host (no value = uses host env)
  AWS_REGION:

env_file:
  - .env          # Load all vars from file
  - .env.local    # Overrides (processed in order)
```

#### Profiles — Conditional Services

```yaml
services:
  api:
    image: myapi
  
  # Only starts when --profile tools is specified
  adminer:
    image: adminer
    profiles: ["tools"]
  
  # Only starts with --profile dev
  mailhog:
    image: mailhog/mailhog
    profiles: ["dev"]
```

```bash
docker compose --profile tools up
docker compose --profile dev up
```

#### Scaling Services

```bash
# Run 3 instances of api
docker compose up --scale api=3
# Note: api must not have a fixed port or only expose (not publish)
```

### Development vs Production Compose Files

Use Compose file inheritance (override files) to share base config:

```bash
# Base: docker-compose.yml (shared config)
# Dev: docker-compose.override.yml (auto-merged with docker compose up)
# Prod: docker-compose.prod.yml

# Development (uses docker-compose.yml + docker-compose.override.yml)
docker compose up

# Production (explicit merge)
docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.prod.yml up -d
```

```yaml
# docker-compose.override.yml (development overrides)
services:
  api:
    build:
      target: development     # Use dev stage
    volumes:
      - ./api/src:/app/src    # Live reload
    environment:
      NODE_ENV: development
    command: npm run dev
  
  db:
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"           # Expose DB locally for tools like TablePlus
```

### Essential Compose Commands

```bash
# Start / Stop
docker compose up -d                    # Start all services, detached
docker compose up -d api db             # Start specific services
docker compose down                     # Stop and remove containers
docker compose down -v                  # Also remove volumes
docker compose stop                     # Stop without removing

# Build
docker compose build                    # Build all services
docker compose build api --no-cache     # Force rebuild
docker compose pull                     # Pull latest images

# Monitoring
docker compose ps                       # Status of all services
docker compose logs -f                  # All logs, follow
docker compose logs -f api --tail 50    # Specific service
docker compose top                      # Running processes

# Execution
docker compose exec api bash            # Shell into running service
docker compose run --rm api npm test    # One-off command

# Lifecycle
docker compose restart api              # Restart service
docker compose pause db / unpause db    # Pause/unpause
```

### Python FastAPI + Postgres Compose

```yaml
services:
  api:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: postgresql+asyncpg://user:pass@db/mydb
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
    volumes:
      - ./app:/app/app  # Hot reload in dev
    command: uvicorn app.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --reload

  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: user
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: pass
      POSTGRES_DB: mydb
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U user -d mydb"]
      interval: 5s
      retries: 5

volumes:
  pgdata:
```

### Interview Talking Points

- "I use service_healthy conditions — not service_started — because the database process starting does not mean it is ready to accept connections."
- "I separate networks: frontend-net for web/API, backend-net marked internal for DB/cache. The DB is never reachable from outside."
- "Dev/prod parity with override files: same base compose.yml, but dev mounts source code for hot reload, prod uses built images."
- "One-shot migrate service with condition: service_completed_successfully ensures migrations run before the API starts."
objectives: Write a complete docker-compose.yml for a multi-tier application
Configure health checks and proper depends_on conditions
Use environment variables securely with .env files
Apply Compose profiles and override files for dev/prod parity

activities.description: Write a docker-compose.yml for a full-stack app: FastAPI backend, PostgreSQL database, Redis cache. Add healthchecks to all stateful services and use service_healthy in depends_on. Start it, verify all services are healthy with 'docker compose ps'.

Add a docker-compose.override.yml that mounts source code for hot reload (development mode) and exposes the database port locally. Verify that running 'docker compose up' uses the override while a separate prod command does not.

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between 'depends_on: service_started' and 'depends_on: service_healthy'?:::service_started (the default) only waits for the container process to be running — Docker considers it started as soon as the container ID exists. service_healthy waits until the container's healthcheck reports 'healthy' status. For databases and caches, the process can start before the service is ready to accept connections. Without service_healthy, your API might try to connect to Postgres before it has finished initialization, causing startup failures. Always use service_healthy for stateful dependencies.

How do you prevent secrets from being committed to git in Docker Compose?:::Never hardcode secrets in docker-compose.yml. Instead: (1) Use ${VARIABLE} interpolation and store values in a .env file. (2) Add .env to .gitignore. (3) Use env_file to load from a separate secrets file. (4) In production, inject environment variables through your deployment platform (Kubernetes Secrets, AWS Secrets Manager, etc.) rather than files. Docker Compose also supports Docker Secrets for Swarm mode.

What does 'internal: true' on a Docker Compose network do?:::An internal network has no gateway to the external internet or host. Containers on an internal network can communicate with each other but cannot make outbound internet calls and cannot be reached from outside Docker. This is a security best practice for backend networks containing databases and caches — even if a container is compromised, it cannot exfiltrate data over the internet.

How would you run database migrations reliably before the API starts in Docker Compose?:::Create a separate migrate service that runs the migration command. Set it to depend on the database with service_healthy. Set the API service to depend on migrate with condition: service_completed_successfully. Set the migrate service to restart: on-failure so it retries if migrations fail transiently. This ensures: (1) DB is healthy before migrations run, (2) migrations complete successfully before API starts, (3) migration failures are retried automatically.
sessions.successCriteria: Can write a production-ready docker-compose.yml with proper health checks, network isolation, secret management, and dev/prod override files

sessions.title: Kubernetes Architecture — Control Plane and Node Components
sessions.content:
## Kubernetes Architecture


```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Control Plane
        API[API Server]
        ETCD[(etcd)]
        SCHED[Scheduler]
        CM[Controller Manager]
    end
    subgraph Worker Node 1
        KL1[kubelet]
        KP1[kube-proxy]
        P1[Pod A]
        P2[Pod B]
    end
    subgraph Worker Node 2
        KL2[kubelet]
        KP2[kube-proxy]
        P3[Pod C]
    end
    API --> ETCD
    API --> SCHED
    API --> CM
    API --> KL1
    API --> KL2
    KL1 --> P1
    KL1 --> P2
    KL2 --> P3
```


Kubernetes (K8s) is a container orchestration platform that automates deployment, scaling, healing, and management of containerized workloads. Understanding its architecture is fundamental to every K8s interview.

### High-Level Architecture

```
+------------------------------------------+
|           CONTROL PLANE (Master)          |
|                                          |
|  kube-apiserver  <-->  etcd              |
|        |                                  |
|  kube-scheduler                          |
|  kube-controller-manager                |
|  cloud-controller-manager               |
+------------------------------------------+
         |          |          |
+--------+  +-------+  +------+
| Node 1  |  | Node 2 |  | Node 3|
|---------|  |--------|  |-------|
| kubelet |  | kubelet|  |kubelet|
|kube-proxy| |kube-prx|  |k-proxy|
|container|  |ctnr rtm|  |ctnr rt|
|runtime  |  |        |  |       |
+--------+  +--------+  +-------+
```

### Control Plane Components

#### 1. kube-apiserver — The Front Door

The API server is the only component that directly reads from and writes to etcd. Every kubectl command, every controller action, every kubelet heartbeat goes through the apiserver. It validates requests, applies admission control, and persists state to etcd.

```bash
# Everything goes through the apiserver
kubectl get pods           # kubectl -> REST -> apiserver -> etcd
kubectl apply -f pod.yaml  # kubectl -> REST -> apiserver -> validates -> etcd
```

Key properties:
- Stateless and horizontally scalable (run multiple replicas)
- Authentication (certs, tokens, OIDC), Authorization (RBAC), Admission control
- Exposes watch API: controllers subscribe to changes

#### 2. etcd — The Brain's Memory

etcd is a distributed, strongly consistent key-value store. It is the single source of truth for all cluster state: nodes, pods, configmaps, secrets, RBAC policies, service accounts.

```
Key format: /registry/{resource-type}/{namespace}/{name}
Example:    /registry/pods/default/myapp-abc123
```

Critical properties:
- Uses Raft consensus — survives N/2 node failures (need 3 or 5 nodes for HA)
- All writes go through the leader; reads can be from any follower (possibly stale)
- Back it up! `etcdctl snapshot save backup.db` — losing etcd = losing the cluster

#### 3. kube-scheduler — The Matchmaker

The scheduler watches for new pods with `nodeName: ""` (unassigned) and selects the best node. It does NOT assign pods directly — it writes the chosen node name to the pod spec via the apiserver.

Scheduling algorithm:
1. **Filtering**: Eliminate nodes that cannot run the pod (insufficient CPU/memory, taints, node selectors, affinity rules)
2. **Scoring**: Rank remaining nodes (spread pods evenly, prefer nodes with pulled image, resource utilization balance)
3. **Binding**: Write the winner's name to the pod spec

```yaml
# Example: Force pod to schedule only on nodes with SSD
spec:
  nodeSelector:
    disktype: ssd

# Taint a node so regular pods cannot schedule there
kubectl taint nodes gpu-node dedicated=gpu:NoSchedule

# Tolerate the taint
spec:
  tolerations:
  - key: dedicated
    operator: Equal
    value: gpu
    effect: NoSchedule
```

#### 4. kube-controller-manager — The Autopilot

Runs a suite of controllers — each watches the apiserver for desired state and reconciles actual state to match. Key controllers:

| Controller | What it does |
|-----------|-------------|
| ReplicaSet | Ensures correct number of pod replicas |
| Deployment | Manages ReplicaSets for rolling updates |
| Node | Marks nodes NotReady when heartbeat stops |
| Job | Manages one-off batch tasks |
| CronJob | Schedules periodic Jobs |
| ServiceAccount | Creates default SAs for new namespaces |
| Namespace | Handles namespace lifecycle |
| PersistentVolume | Binds PVCs to PVs |

Every controller runs a control loop:
```
while True:
  desired_state = watch_apiserver()
  actual_state  = observe_cluster()
  if desired != actual:
    reconcile()
```

#### 5. cloud-controller-manager

Interfaces with cloud provider APIs. When you create a `LoadBalancer` Service, the cloud-controller-manager calls AWS/GCP/Azure APIs to provision the actual load balancer. Decouples K8s from cloud-specific code.

### Worker Node Components

#### kubelet — The Node Agent

Runs on every worker node. Its job: make sure containers in pods are running as specified.

- Registers the node with the apiserver
- Watches for pods assigned to its node
- Pulls images and starts containers via the container runtime
- Reports pod status and node health back to apiserver
- Runs liveness/readiness probes
- Does NOT manage pods started outside Kubernetes

#### kube-proxy — The Network Rules Engine

Runs on every node. Maintains iptables (or IPVS) rules that implement Service load balancing. When a pod sends a request to a Service ClusterIP, kube-proxy's rules intercept and forward it to one of the backend pods.

```bash
# kube-proxy in iptables mode creates rules like:
# Service ClusterIP 10.96.0.10:80 -> randomly pick one of:
#   Pod 10.244.1.5:8080 (33%)
#   Pod 10.244.2.3:8080 (33%)
#   Pod 10.244.3.7:8080 (33%)
```

#### Container Runtime Interface (CRI)

K8s is runtime-agnostic. It communicates with any CRI-compliant runtime:
- **containerd**: Default in modern K8s (what Docker uses under the hood)
- **CRI-O**: Lightweight, OCI-compliant
- **Docker Engine**: Deprecated in K8s 1.24+

### Namespaces — Logical Cluster Partitioning

```bash
# Built-in namespaces
kube-system    # K8s system components (apiserver, coredns, kube-proxy)
kube-public    # Readable by all users (cluster info)
kube-node-lease # Node heartbeat leases
default        # Where your stuff goes if you don't specify

# Create and use namespaces
kubectl create namespace staging
kubectl get pods -n staging
kubectl get pods --all-namespaces
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=staging  # Change default
```

### How a Pod Gets Scheduled — End to End

```
1. kubectl apply -f pod.yaml
2. API server validates, persists to etcd, returns 201
3. Scheduler watches etcd — sees pod with nodeName: ""
4. Scheduler filters + scores nodes, picks "node-2"
5. Scheduler writes nodeName: node-2 to pod spec via apiserver
6. kubelet on node-2 watches apiserver, sees pod assigned to it
7. kubelet calls container runtime to pull image
8. kubelet starts container, reports back: phase = Running
9. controller-manager watches — confirms replica count matches desired
```

### Interview Talking Points

- "The apiserver is stateless — all state lives in etcd. This is why losing etcd means losing the cluster."
- "The scheduler does not start containers — it only writes a nodeName to the pod spec. The kubelet does the actual work."
- "Every controller is an independent loop: watch desired, observe actual, reconcile. This is the fundamental K8s pattern."
- "kube-proxy implements Services — without it, ClusterIP routing stops working."
objectives: Name and explain all control plane components and their responsibilities
Describe what happens step-by-step when a pod is scheduled
Explain the role of kubelet and kube-proxy on worker nodes
Understand how controllers implement the reconciliation loop

activities.description: On a local cluster (minikube or kind), run 'kubectl get pods -n kube-system' and identify all control plane components. Use 'kubectl describe pod kube-scheduler-... -n kube-system' to examine the scheduler configuration.
Draw the complete K8s architecture from memory: control plane components, worker node components, and the flow of a kubectl apply command from client to running container. Verify against the lesson.

reviewQuestions: What is etcd and why is it critical to Kubernetes?:::etcd is a distributed key-value store that serves as the single source of truth for all Kubernetes state — every pod, service, configmap, secret, node, and RBAC policy is stored there. The kube-apiserver is the only component that reads from and writes to etcd. If etcd is lost, the entire cluster state is lost — you cannot recover which pods were running, what services existed, or any configuration. This is why etcd backup is mandatory in production. etcd uses the Raft consensus algorithm, requiring a quorum (N/2 + 1 nodes), so production clusters run 3 or 5 etcd members.

What does the kube-scheduler actually do when it schedules a pod?:::The scheduler watches for pods with an empty nodeName field. It runs two phases: Filtering removes nodes that cannot accommodate the pod (insufficient CPU/memory, incompatible node selectors, taints without matching tolerations, pod affinity violations). Scoring ranks the remaining nodes using plugins that consider resource balance, image locality, and spread. The scheduler then writes the selected node's name into the pod spec via the apiserver. It does NOT contact the kubelet or start any container — that is the kubelet's job after it sees the updated pod spec.

What is the difference between kube-controller-manager and kubelet?:::The kube-controller-manager runs on the control plane and manages cluster-level reconciliation loops — it watches the desired state (e.g., 'I want 3 replicas') and creates/deletes pods to match. The kubelet runs on every worker node and is responsible for actually running the containers assigned to that node. The kubelet contacts the container runtime (containerd) to pull images and start processes. Think of it this way: the controller-manager decides WHAT should run WHERE, and the kubelet makes it happen on its specific node.

Why was the Docker runtime deprecated in Kubernetes 1.24?:::Kubernetes uses a Container Runtime Interface (CRI) to communicate with container runtimes in a standardized way. Docker Engine does not natively implement CRI — K8s used a shim called 'dockershim' to bridge the gap. This shim added complexity, had a maintenance burden, and was slower than native CRI runtimes. Docker Engine itself uses containerd under the hood, so K8s can talk directly to containerd via CRI and get the same functionality without the Docker layer. Container images built with Docker are still fully compatible — only the runtime daemon changed.
sessions.successCriteria: Can explain all K8s control plane and node components from memory, describe the pod scheduling flow end-to-end, and articulate the reconciliation loop pattern

sessions.title: Pods, ReplicaSets & Deployments — Running Workloads
sessions.content:
## Pods, ReplicaSets & Deployments

The Pod is the atomic unit of scheduling in Kubernetes. Deployments manage Pods at scale with rolling updates and rollbacks. Understanding this hierarchy is essential for every K8s interview.

### Pod — The Atomic Unit

A Pod is a group of one or more containers that share:
- **Network namespace**: Same IP address and port space
- **Storage volumes**: Can share volumes between containers
- **Lifecycle**: Scheduled and terminated together

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: myapi
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: myapi
    version: v1
spec:
  containers:
  - name: api
    image: myapi:1.0
    ports:
    - containerPort: 3000
    env:
    - name: NODE_ENV
      value: production
    - name: DB_PASSWORD
      valueFrom:
        secretKeyRef:
          name: db-secret
          key: password
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: 100m       # 100 millicores = 0.1 CPU
        memory: 128Mi
      limits:
        cpu: 500m       # Max 0.5 CPU (throttled if exceeded)
        memory: 512Mi   # Max 512MB (OOMKilled if exceeded)
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /health
        port: 3000
      initialDelaySeconds: 5
      periodSeconds: 10
      failureThreshold: 3
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /health
        port: 3000
      initialDelaySeconds: 15
      periodSeconds: 20
      failureThreshold: 3
  restartPolicy: Always
```

### Probes — The Heartbeat System

| Probe | Purpose | On Failure |
|-------|---------|------------|
| **readinessProbe** | Is the pod ready to receive traffic? | Remove from Service endpoints |
| **livenessProbe** | Is the container stuck/crashed? | Restart the container |
| **startupProbe** | Has the app finished starting? | Restart if takes too long |

```yaml
# Probe types
readinessProbe:
  httpGet:           # HTTP GET — passes if 200-399
    path: /ready
    port: 8080
  
  tcpSocket:         # TCP connect — passes if connection accepted
    port: 5432
  
  exec:              # Run command — passes if exit code 0
    command: ["redis-cli", "ping"]
```

### Multi-Container Pod Patterns

#### Sidecar Pattern
Auxiliary container that enhances the main container.

```yaml
spec:
  containers:
  - name: api
    image: myapi:1.0
    volumeMounts:
    - name: logs
      mountPath: /app/logs
  
  - name: log-shipper          # Sidecar: ships logs to Elasticsearch
    image: fluent-bit:latest
    volumeMounts:
    - name: logs
      mountPath: /var/log/app
  
  volumes:
  - name: logs
    emptyDir: {}               # Shared ephemeral volume
```

#### Init Container Pattern
Runs to completion BEFORE main containers start.

```yaml
spec:
  initContainers:
  - name: wait-for-db
    image: busybox
    command: ['sh', '-c', 'until nc -z db-service 5432; do sleep 2; done']
  
  - name: run-migrations
    image: myapi:1.0
    command: ['npm', 'run', 'migrate']
    env:
    - name: DATABASE_URL
      valueFrom:
        secretKeyRef: {name: db-secret, key: url}
  
  containers:
  - name: api
    image: myapi:1.0
    # Only starts after both init containers complete
```

### Pod Lifecycle

```
Pending -> Running -> Succeeded (for Jobs)
                  -> Failed
       -> Unknown
```

- **Pending**: Pod accepted, waiting for scheduling or image pull
- **Running**: At least one container is running
- **Succeeded**: All containers exited 0 (Jobs)
- **Failed**: All containers have exited, at least one with non-zero
- **Unknown**: Node communication lost

### ReplicaSet — Ensuring Pod Count

A ReplicaSet ensures a specified number of pod replicas are running at all times. You rarely create ReplicaSets directly — Deployments manage them.

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: ReplicaSet
metadata:
  name: myapi-rs
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: myapi     # Manages pods with this label
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: myapi   # Must match selector
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: api
        image: myapi:1.0
```

If a pod is deleted or crashes, the ReplicaSet controller creates a replacement. If you add a pod manually with the label `app: myapi`, the ReplicaSet will delete it (too many replicas).

### Deployment — Rolling Updates and Rollbacks

A Deployment manages ReplicaSets, enabling declarative updates.

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: myapi
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 4
  revisionHistoryLimit: 5    # Keep 5 old ReplicaSets for rollback
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: myapi
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1            # Max pods ABOVE desired count during update
      maxUnavailable: 0      # Max pods that can be unavailable during update
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: myapi
        version: v2
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: api
        image: myapi:2.0
        resources:
          requests: {cpu: 100m, memory: 128Mi}
          limits: {cpu: 500m, memory: 512Mi}
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet: {path: /health, port: 3000}
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
```

### Rolling Update Deep Dive

With `replicas: 4`, `maxSurge: 1`, `maxUnavailable: 0`:

```
Step 1: Create 1 new pod (v2)     [3 old, 1 new = 4 total, 4 available]
Step 2: Wait for new pod Ready    [3 old, 1 new-ready = 4 available]
Step 3: Terminate 1 old pod       [2 old, 1 new = 3 total, 3 available]
Step 4: Create 1 new pod (v2)     [2 old, 2 new = 4 total, 3 available]
Step 5: Wait for new pod Ready    [2 old, 2 new-ready = 4 available]
...repeat until all 4 are v2
```

Zero downtime because `maxUnavailable: 0` ensures at least 4 pods are always available.

### Deployment Commands

```bash
# Deploy and update
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
kubectl set image deployment/myapi api=myapi:2.0  # Quick image update
kubectl set env deployment/myapi NODE_ENV=production

# Monitor rollout
kubectl rollout status deployment/myapi
kubectl rollout history deployment/myapi
kubectl rollout history deployment/myapi --revision=3

# Rollback
kubectl rollout undo deployment/myapi              # Previous revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/myapi --to-revision=2  # Specific revision

# Scaling
kubectl scale deployment myapi --replicas=8
kubectl autoscale deployment myapi --min=2 --max=10 --cpu-percent=70

# Pause/resume (batch multiple changes without triggering rollout each time)
kubectl rollout pause deployment/myapi
kubectl set image deployment/myapi api=myapi:2.0
kubectl set env deployment/myapi LOG_LEVEL=debug
kubectl rollout resume deployment/myapi  # Now triggers one rollout
```

### Interview Talking Points

- "Readiness vs liveness: readiness controls traffic routing, liveness controls restarts. Misconfiguring these is a common cause of production incidents."
- "maxSurge: 1, maxUnavailable: 0 is my default — it guarantees zero downtime but requires one extra pod worth of resources during the update."
- "Init containers are great for: waiting for dependencies, running migrations, fetching secrets, and seeding config files."
- "A ReplicaSet adopts any existing pod matching its label selector — which can cause surprises if you're not careful with labels."
objectives: Write a complete Pod spec with resources, probes, and environment variables
Explain readinessProbe vs livenessProbe and when each triggers
Describe how rolling updates work with maxSurge and maxUnavailable
Use kubectl to deploy, update, monitor, and roll back a Deployment

activities.description: Deploy a simple web app Deployment with 3 replicas, readiness and liveness probes, and resource limits. Use 'kubectl rollout status' to watch it come up. Then update the image tag and watch the rolling update. Deliberately trigger a rollback by setting an invalid image tag.

Create a multi-container pod with a main app container and a sidecar that writes to a shared emptyDir volume. Verify both containers are running and the sidecar can read the main container's output via the shared volume.

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between a readinessProbe and a livenessProbe?:::readinessProbe determines whether a pod should receive traffic from a Service. If readiness fails, the pod is removed from the Service's endpoint list but continues running — no restart. This is used for: slow startup, temporarily overloaded instances, or maintenance mode. livenessProbe determines whether a container is still alive. If liveness fails repeatedly (failureThreshold times), kubelet restarts the container. Use liveness for detecting deadlocks or hung processes. Wrong configuration: using livenessProbe for startup checks (causes restart loop) or using readinessProbe when you need a restart (pod stays alive but receives no traffic).

Why do you almost never create ReplicaSets directly?:::You create Deployments instead, because Deployments manage ReplicaSets for you and add critical features on top: rolling updates (Deployment creates a new RS, scales it up while scaling down the old RS), rollback history (keeps old ReplicaSets with their pod templates so you can roll back), and pause/resume (batch multiple spec changes into one rollout). If you create a ReplicaSet directly, you get no update strategy — you'd have to delete and recreate pods manually.

Explain what happens during a rolling update with maxSurge: 1 and maxUnavailable: 0 on a 3-replica Deployment.:::With 3 desired replicas, maxSurge: 1 allows up to 4 pods (3+1) total. maxUnavailable: 0 means at least 3 pods must be available at all times. The update proceeds: (1) Create 1 new pod -> 4 total, 3 available (new not ready yet). (2) Wait for new pod to pass readinessProbe -> 4 total, 4 available. (3) Terminate 1 old pod -> 3 total, 3 available. (4) Create 1 new pod -> 4 total, 3 available. Repeat until all 3 are updated. Zero downtime guaranteed because available count never drops below 3.

What are init containers and what are they good for?:::Init containers run to completion before any main containers start. Multiple init containers run sequentially in order. If any fails, kubelet restarts it (or the whole pod depending on restartPolicy). Common uses: (1) Wait for a dependency to be ready (loop until database TCP port accepts connections). (2) Run database migrations before the app starts. (3) Download or generate config files and place them on a shared volume. (4) Register the pod with an external service. They are better than startup scripts in the main container because they can use different images (e.g., a migration image vs the app image) and failures do not pollute the main container's restart count.
sessions.successCriteria: Can write Pod specs with proper probes and resource limits, explain the rolling update algorithm, and perform deployments, updates, and rollbacks using kubectl

sessions.title: Services, Ingress & Networking — Exposing Applications
sessions.content:
## Services, Ingress & Networking ```mermaid graph LR Client[Client] --> ING[Ingress
nginx / traefik] ING -->|/api| SVC1[Service: api
ClusterIP] ING -->|/web| SVC2[Service: web
ClusterIP] SVC1 --> P1[Pod api-1] SVC1 --> P2[Pod api-2] SVC2 --> P3[Pod web-1] SVC2 --> P4[Pod web-2] ``` Pods are ephemeral — they get new IP addresses when restarted. Services provide stable endpoints. Ingress provides HTTP routing from outside the cluster. Together they form Kubernetes' networking model. ### Why Services Exist Problems without Services: - Pod IPs change on every restart - Multiple pods (replicas) need load balancing - Pods cannot discover each other reliably Services solve this by providing a stable virtual IP (ClusterIP) and DNS name that load balances across all matching pods. ### Service Types #### 1. ClusterIP (Default) — Internal Only ```yaml apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: api-service namespace: default spec: type: ClusterIP # Default if not specified selector: app: myapi # Routes to pods with this label ports: - name: http port: 80 # Service port (what clients connect to) targetPort: 3000 # Container port (where app listens) protocol: TCP ``` DNS: `api-service.default.svc.cluster.local` (or just `api-service` within same namespace) ```bash # From another pod in the same namespace: curl http://api-service/users # From another namespace: curl http://api-service.default.svc.cluster.local/users ``` #### 2. NodePort — External via Node IP ```yaml spec: type: NodePort selector: app: myapi ports: - port: 80 targetPort: 3000 nodePort: 30080 # Optional: fixed port 30000-32767, random if omitted ``` Access: `http://:30080` — useful for dev/testing, not production. #### 3. LoadBalancer — Cloud-Native External Access ```yaml spec: type: LoadBalancer selector: app: myapi ports: - port: 80 targetPort: 3000 # Optionally pin cloud LB settings: annotations: service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type: nlb service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-internal: 'true' ``` The cloud-controller-manager provisions an AWS ALB/NLB, GCP LB, or Azure LB and assigns an external IP. Creates NodePort internally. #### 4. ExternalName — DNS Alias to External Service ```yaml spec: type: ExternalName externalName: my-database.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com ``` Allows in-cluster services to reach external services by K8s DNS name. No proxy — pure DNS CNAME. #### 5. Headless Service — Direct Pod DNS ```yaml spec: clusterIP: None # Headless: no virtual IP selector: app: cassandra ``` DNS returns the IPs of all matching pods directly. Used by StatefulSets so each pod (cassandra-0, cassandra-1) gets a stable DNS name. ### Ingress — HTTP/S Routing One LoadBalancer Service per app is expensive in cloud environments. Ingress lets one load balancer route to many services based on hostname and path. #### Ingress Resource ```yaml apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: Ingress metadata: name: app-ingress annotations: nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: / nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: 'true' nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rate-limit: '100' spec: ingressClassName: nginx # Which controller handles this tls: - hosts: - api.example.com - app.example.com secretName: tls-secret # TLS cert stored as K8s Secret rules: - host: api.example.com http: paths: - path: /v1 pathType: Prefix backend: service: name: api-v1-service port: {number: 80} - path: /v2 pathType: Prefix backend: service: name: api-v2-service port: {number: 80} - host: app.example.com http: paths: - path: / pathType: Prefix backend: service: name: frontend-service port: {number: 80} ``` #### Popular Ingress Controllers | Controller | Best For | |-----------|----------| | nginx-ingress | General purpose, most widely used | | Traefik | Automatic TLS, dynamic config | | AWS ALB Ingress | Native AWS integration | | GCE Ingress | GCP native | | Istio Gateway | Service mesh, advanced traffic management | ```bash # Install nginx-ingress controller with Helm helm repo add ingress-nginx https://kubernetes.github.io/ingress-nginx helm install ingress-nginx ingress-nginx/ingress-nginx \ --namespace ingress-nginx \ --create-namespace ``` ### Kubernetes DNS — CoreDNS CoreDNS runs as a Deployment in `kube-system`. Every pod automatically has DNS configured to use it. ``` Service DNS format: ..svc.cluster.local Pod DNS format (headless): ..pod.cluster.local Examples: api-service.production.svc.cluster.local 10-244-1-5.default.pod.cluster.local Short forms within same namespace: api-service # Works within same namespace api-service.production # Works from any namespace ``` ### NetworkPolicy — Pod-Level Firewall By default, all pods can communicate with all other pods. NetworkPolicy restricts this. ```yaml apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: NetworkPolicy metadata: name: api-netpol namespace: production spec: podSelector: matchLabels: app: myapi # Apply to pods with app=myapi policyTypes: - Ingress - Egress ingress: - from: - namespaceSelector: matchLabels: name: production # Only from production namespace - podSelector: matchLabels: app: nginx # Only from nginx pods ports: - protocol: TCP port: 3000 egress: - to: - podSelector: matchLabels: app: db # Can only reach DB pods ports: - protocol: TCP port: 5432 ``` Note: NetworkPolicy requires a CNI plugin that supports it (Calico, Cilium, Weave). The default K8s networking (kubenet) does not enforce NetworkPolicies. ### Real-World Architecture Pattern ``` Internet | [Cloud LB] <-- LoadBalancer Service: ingress-nginx | [nginx Ingress Controller Pod] | +-- api.example.com/v1 --> [ClusterIP: api-v1-svc] --> [api-v1 pods x3] +-- api.example.com/v2 --> [ClusterIP: api-v2-svc] --> [api-v2 pods x3] +-- app.example.com --> [ClusterIP: frontend-svc] --> [frontend pods x2] [api pods] --> [ClusterIP: db-svc] --> [postgres pod] (via NetworkPolicy) ``` ### Essential Service Commands ```bash # View services kubectl get services kubectl get svc -n production kubectl describe svc api-service # Endpoints (which pods a service routes to) kubectl get endpoints api-service # Port forward for local testing (no Ingress needed) kubectl port-forward svc/api-service 8080:80 kubectl port-forward pod/mypod 3000:3000 # DNS debugging kubectl run -it --rm dns-test --image=busybox -- nslookup api-service kubectl run -it --rm curl-test --image=curlimages/curl -- curl http://api-service/health # Ingress kubectl get ingress kubectl describe ingress app-ingress ``` ### Interview Talking Points - "ClusterIP for internal service-to-service communication. LoadBalancer or Ingress for external traffic — Ingress is preferred because you don't pay for one LB per service." - "Ingress is just config — it does nothing without an Ingress controller. I always install nginx-ingress or Traefik first." - "CoreDNS makes service discovery automatic. Apps don't need service registries — just use the service name as the hostname." - "NetworkPolicy is the K8s firewall. Default: all pods can reach all pods. In production I add zero-trust NetworkPolicies to each namespace."
objectives: Explain all five Service types and their appropriate use cases
Write Ingress resources with host-based and path-based routing, TLS
Understand CoreDNS service discovery and DNS naming conventions
Write NetworkPolicy manifests to implement zero-trust pod networking

activities.description: Deploy two versions of an app (v1, v2) as separate Deployments, each with its own ClusterIP Service. Create an Ingress that routes /v1 to the first Service and /v2 to the second. Use port-forward to test before the Ingress is set up, then test via Ingress.

Write a NetworkPolicy for an API pod that: allows ingress only from nginx pods on port 3000, and allows egress only to database pods on port 5432. Apply it and verify that a test pod without the nginx label cannot reach the API.

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between a NodePort and a LoadBalancer Service?:::NodePort opens a static port (30000-32767) on EVERY node's external IP. To reach it, you need to know a node's IP and the assigned port. It is simple but requires external load balancing in front of the nodes, and port numbers are not user-friendly. LoadBalancer builds on NodePort and additionally provisions a cloud provider's external load balancer (AWS ALB/NLB, GCP LB, Azure LB) via the cloud-controller-manager. The cloud LB gets a stable external IP/hostname and forwards to the NodePort. LoadBalancer is the production standard for cloud-hosted K8s. On-premises clusters use MetalLB to emulate LoadBalancer behavior.

What is the difference between an Ingress resource and an Ingress controller?:::An Ingress resource is a Kubernetes API object (YAML manifest) that defines HTTP routing rules: which hostnames and URL paths map to which backend Services. It is pure configuration — like a routing table. An Ingress controller is the actual software that reads Ingress resources and implements the rules by configuring a reverse proxy (nginx, Traefik, HAProxy). Without a controller, Ingress resources have zero effect. The controller runs as a Deployment in the cluster (typically in ingress-nginx namespace) and typically has ONE LoadBalancer Service in front of it — this is how you go from one cloud LB to routing many services.

How does Kubernetes DNS work, and how would a pod in namespace 'staging' reach a service named 'api-service' in namespace 'production'?:::CoreDNS runs as a Deployment in kube-system and handles all DNS for the cluster. Every pod is configured to use CoreDNS as its DNS server. Services get DNS records in the format: <service>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local. From the staging namespace, you can use the short form 'api-service' only if the service is in the same namespace. To reach a service in a different namespace, you must use the fully qualified name or at minimum include the namespace: 'api-service.production' or 'api-service.production.svc.cluster.local'. This cross-namespace DNS is how microservices in different teams' namespaces communicate.

What is a headless Service and when would you use it?:::A headless Service is a ClusterIP Service where clusterIP is set to None. Instead of a virtual IP with load balancing, DNS returns the individual IP addresses of all matching pods. This allows clients to implement their own load balancing or connect to a specific pod. Primary use case: StatefulSets for databases. Each pod (mysql-0, mysql-1, mysql-2) gets a stable DNS name via the headless service: mysql-0.mysql-headless.default.svc.cluster.local. The primary database can be addressed specifically rather than randomly. Cassandra and Kafka pods use headless services so clients can discover the full list of nodes for gossip protocol and partition assignment.
sessions.successCriteria: Can write Service and Ingress manifests for a production-grade routing setup, explain DNS-based service discovery, and implement NetworkPolicies for zero-trust pod communication

sessions.title: ConfigMaps, Secrets & Environment Variables
sessions.content:
## ConfigMaps, Secrets & Environment Variables

Decoupling configuration from container images is a foundational Kubernetes practice. ConfigMaps store non-sensitive configuration; Secrets store sensitive data (base64-encoded at rest by default, optionally encrypted with KMS).

### Creating ConfigMaps

```bash
# Imperative: from literal values
kubectl create configmap app-config \
  --from-literal=APP_ENV=production \
  --from-literal=LOG_LEVEL=info

# Imperative: from a file
kubectl create configmap nginx-conf --from-file=nginx.conf

# View the result
kubectl get configmap app-config -o yaml
kubectl describe configmap app-config
```

### ConfigMap YAML Manifest

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
  namespace: production
data:
  APP_ENV: production
  LOG_LEVEL: info
  DATABASE_HOST: postgres-service.production.svc.cluster.local
  config.properties: |
    server.port=8080
    cache.ttl=300
    feature.new_ui=true
```

### Creating Secrets

```bash
# Imperative: generic secret
kubectl create secret generic db-credentials \
  --from-literal=username=admin \
  --from-literal=password='S3cr3tP@ss!'

# TLS secret from cert files
kubectl create secret tls tls-cert \
  --cert=tls.crt --key=tls.key

# Docker registry credentials
kubectl create secret docker-registry regcred \
  --docker-server=registry.example.com \
  --docker-username=myuser \
  --docker-password=mypassword
```

### Secret YAML (values are base64-encoded)

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: db-credentials
type: Opaque
data:
  username: YWRtaW4=        # echo -n 'admin' | base64
  password: UzNjcjN0UEBzcyE=
stringData:                  # plaintext — K8s encodes automatically
  api_key: "my-secret-api-key"
```

### Consuming ConfigMaps & Secrets: Environment Variables

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: myapp
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: myapp:1.0
        env:
        - name: APP_ENV
          valueFrom:
            configMapKeyRef:
              name: app-config
              key: APP_ENV
        - name: DB_PASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: db-credentials
              key: password
        envFrom:              # Load ALL keys as env vars
        - configMapRef:
            name: app-config
        - secretRef:
            name: db-credentials
```

### Consuming ConfigMaps & Secrets: Volume Mounts

```yaml
        volumeMounts:
        - name: config-vol
          mountPath: /etc/config
          readOnly: true
        - name: secret-vol
          mountPath: /etc/secrets
          readOnly: true
      volumes:
      - name: config-vol
        configMap:
          name: app-config
          items:
          - key: config.properties
            path: app.properties
      - name: secret-vol
        secret:
          secretName: db-credentials
          defaultMode: 0400   # Restrict permissions
```

When mounted as volumes, changes to a ConfigMap are reflected in the pod automatically (within ~60 seconds) — no restart required. Secrets mounted as volumes also auto-update.

### Interview Tips
- Never bake secrets into images or ConfigMaps — use Secrets with RBAC to restrict access.
- In production, use an external secret manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) with the External Secrets Operator.
- `stringData` in Secret manifests is write-only; reads always show base64 `data`.
- Secrets are only base64-encoded by default, not encrypted — enable Encryption at Rest in the API server config for real security.

```bash
# Decode a secret
kubectl get secret db-credentials -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 -d

# Watch for ConfigMap changes
kubectl get configmap app-config -w
```
